title: Guillain-Barré Syndrome in a 42-Year-Old Man with Rapidly Ascending Symmetric Weakness and Areflexia Two Weeks After a Diarrheal Illness: Serial Respiratory Monitoring (Forced Vital Capacity / Negative Inspiratory Force) to Anticipate Neuromuscular Respiratory Failure, Recognizing Dysautonomia, IVIG or Plasmapheresis, and the Comprehensive Supportive Care of an Awake, Progressively Paralyzed Patient (Airway, Immobility, Communication, Nutrition, Pain, and Psychological Support)
subtitle: GUILLAIN-BARRÉ SYNDROME (GBS) = an acute, immune-mediated polyradiculoneuropathy (most commonly acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy/AIDP) in which an aberrant immune response—frequently triggered ~1-3 WEEKS after an infection (classically CAMPYLOBACTER JEJUNI gastroenteritis; also CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma, influenza/URI, Zika; rarely post-vaccination) via MOLECULAR MIMICRY—attacks peripheral nerve myelin (and sometimes axons). CLASSIC PRESENTATION: rapidly progressive, SYMMETRIC, ASCENDING weakness/flaccid paralysis that typically begins in the LEGS and ascends over hours-to-days to the trunk, arms, respiratory muscles, and cranial nerves, with HYPOREFLEXIA/AREFLEXIA (loss of deep tendon reflexes — a hallmark), often preceded/accompanied by distal PARESTHESIAS; motor involvement usually dominates (sensory symptoms milder); progression peaks within ~2-4 weeks, then a plateau, then gradual recovery. THE TWO LIFE-THREATS the nurse must vigilantly monitor: (1) NEUROMUSCULAR RESPIRATORY FAILURE — as the paralysis ASCENDS to the diaphragm/intercostals it causes ventilatory failure (the leading cause of death); MONITOR SERIAL bedside RESPIRATORY MECHANICS — FORCED VITAL CAPACITY (FVC) and NEGATIVE INSPIRATORY FORCE (NIF/maximal inspiratory pressure) — at scheduled intervals; a FALLING FVC (e.g., <15-20 mL/kg or <~1 L, or a rapid downward trend) and a weakening NIF herald impending respiratory failure and the need to ELECTIVELY intubate/ventilate BEFORE a crisis—do NOT wait for hypoxemia or a rising CO2 on ABG, which are LATE signs; other bedside clues include a rising RR, shallow breathing, accessory-muscle use, a weak cough, difficulty handling secretions, inability to complete a sentence, and a falling single-breath count; bulbar weakness (dysphagia, weak cough, facial weakness) adds ASPIRATION risk. (2) AUTONOMIC DYSFUNCTION (DYSAUTONOMIA) — labile/wide swings in BLOOD PRESSURE (hyper- and hypotension), CARDIAC ARRHYTHMIAS (tachy- and bradyarrhythmias, even asystole), ileus, urinary retention, and diaphoresis → continuous CARDIAC + BP monitoring, cautious response to hemodynamic swings, and avoiding/anticipating triggers. DIAGNOSIS: largely clinical, supported by LUMBAR PUNCTURE showing ALBUMINOCYTOLOGIC DISSOCIATION (elevated CSF PROTEIN with a NORMAL/near-normal cell count) and by NERVE CONDUCTION STUDIES/EMG. TREATMENT (disease-modifying): IVIG (intravenous immunoglobulin) OR PLASMAPHERESIS (plasma exchange) — they are EQUALLY effective and are NOT combined; started early shortens the course; CORTICOSTEROIDS are NOT effective in GBS (an important distinction from many autoimmune conditions). Otherwise, meticulous SUPPORTIVE CARE is the mainstay and determines outcome. PROGNOSIS: usually MONOPHASIC; most patients recover substantially over weeks-to-months (recovery typically proceeds proximal-to-distal—roughly the reverse of onset); many regain near-baseline function, though some have residual weakness/fatigue; mortality (~3-7%) stems from respiratory failure, dysautonomia (arrhythmia), PE, sepsis, and other complications of prolonged immobility. COMPREHENSIVE NURSING/SUPPORTIVE CARE (Basic-Care-&-Comfort intensive, because the patient is typically FULLY AWAKE, ALERT, and able to FEEL while becoming unable to move or speak): AIRWAY/RESPIRATORY—serial FVC/NIF, suctioning, aspiration precautions/swallow assessment, ventilator care if intubated; IMMOBILITY care—VTE/DVT prophylaxis (pharmacologic + sequential compression devices), PRESSURE-INJURY prevention (repositioning q2h, support surfaces, skin assessment), passive RANGE-OF-MOTION + proper POSITIONING/splints to prevent CONTRACTURES and footdrop; NUTRITION—assess swallowing (high aspiration risk with bulbar involvement; NPO + enteral feeding if unsafe), adequate calories/protein for healing, bowel regimen for ileus/constipation; ELIMINATION—monitor for urinary retention (bladder scan/intermittent catheterization) and ileus; PAIN—neuropathic and musculoskeletal/back pain are common and often severe → gabapentin/pregabalin + analgesia + repositioning; EYE CARE—if facial weakness causes incomplete lid closure, lubricate/protect the cornea; COMMUNICATION—establish a reliable method (yes/no signals, eye blinks, letter/picture boards, communication devices) for a patient who may be unable to speak or move but is fully conscious; explain ALL care, never speak about the patient as if absent, and orient frequently; PSYCHOLOGICAL/EMOTIONAL support—being awake and progressively paralyzed (possibly ventilated) is TERRIFYING → provide constant reassurance (emphasizing that GBS is usually REVERSIBLE with recovery expected), reduce isolation, screen for anxiety/depression, involve family, and engage chaplain/psychology; INFECTION prevention (pneumonia, UTI, line/skin infections). MULTIDISCIPLINARY: neurology, critical care, RESPIRATORY THERAPY, PT/OT/SPEECH therapy, dietitian, social work, and rehabilitation (recovery is gradual and rehab-intensive). NURSING ROLE: above all, monitor the trajectory of the ascending paralysis and the SERIAL respiratory mechanics to anticipate respiratory failure BEFORE it becomes a crisis; monitor for dysautonomia; deliver disease-modifying therapy (IVIG/plasmapheresis); and provide the comprehensive, comfort-focused, communication-centered supportive care that protects an awake, paralyzed patient from the complications of immobility and from fear

patient.name: R. Haddad
patient.code: Full code; fully alert with intact cognition + decision-making capacity (GBS does not impair consciousness/cognition); wife present; goals = prevent/manage respiratory failure, support him through the paralysis, treat with IVIG, and recover function
patient.admitDate: Presented after ~5 days of progressive symmetric weakness that began as tingling and weakness in both feet and has ASCENDED to involve both legs (now unable to walk) and is now affecting his hands, ~2 weeks after a self-limited diarrheal illness (presumed Campylobacter). On exam he has symmetric flaccid lower-extremity weakness, early upper-extremity weakness, and ABSENT deep tendon reflexes (areflexia), with intact alertness/cognition. He is anxious and frightened by the rapid progression. Given the ascending course and risk of respiratory + autonomic compromise, he is admitted to a monitored setting for serial respiratory mechanics, cardiac/BP monitoring, diagnostic confirmation, IVIG, and comprehensive supportive care.
patient.room: Step-down/ICU-capable monitored bed with continuous cardiac + SpO2 monitoring; airway/intubation equipment readily available; multidisciplinary team (neurology, critical care, respiratory therapy, PT/OT/speech, dietitian, social work); wife at bedside; calm, communication-focused environment

vitals.temp: 37.0°C; fully alert + oriented + anxious; symmetric flaccid weakness of both legs (cannot stand) with early hand weakness; AREFLEXIA (absent DTRs); distal paresthesias; cough present but assess strength; baseline bedside FORCED VITAL CAPACITY (FVC) and NEGATIVE INSPIRATORY FORCE (NIF) obtained + single-breath count documented as the trend baseline; swallow screened

37.0°C; weakness has ascended further (now mid-thigh + both hands); SERIAL FVC and NIF are TRENDING DOWN from baseline; single-breath count decreasing; weaker cough + beginning to have difficulty clearing secretions; SpO2 still adequate (a falling FVC/NIF precedes hypoxemia) → neurology/critical care notified, airway readiness confirmed, ICU transfer arranged for likely elective intubation; IVIG initiated; cardiac monitoring for dysautonomia

vitals.bp: labile (160/95 ↔ 100/60); transient bradycardia noted with suctioning (autonomic)
vitals.rr: ventilator-controlled after ELECTIVE intubation (FVC fell below threshold + weak cough + secretion difficulty) — intubated in a controlled manner BEFORE respiratory arrest/hypoxemia
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; electively intubated/ventilated for neuromuscular respiratory failure (anticipated from the declining FVC/NIF, not from late hypoxemia); fully AWAKE + able to feel → communication method established (eye blinks/board) + everything explained; continuous cardiac + BP monitoring for dysautonomia (cautious with suctioning-induced bradycardia); IVIG continuing; comprehensive supportive care begun

37.1°C; at the plateau of paralysis (near-complete tetraparesis) but fully conscious; comprehensive Basic-Care-&-Comfort measures in full effect — VTE prophylaxis + SCDs, q2h repositioning + skin/pressure-injury surveillance, passive ROM + splints (prevent contractures/footdrop), enteral nutrition (unsafe swallow), bowel/bladder management, neuropathic + back PAIN controlled (gabapentin + analgesia), eye lubrication for facial weakness, consistent COMMUNICATION + reassurance + family presence; IVIG course completed; monitoring for infection/complications

vitals.rr: weaning — improving spontaneous respiratory effort; FVC/NIF trending UP
vitals.temp: 36.9°C; beginning to recover — respiratory mechanics improving (FVC/NIF rising), some return of proximal movement (recovery proceeds proximal-to-distal, reverse of onset); progressing through ventilator weaning trials; continuing supportive care + early rehabilitation (PT/OT/speech); no pressure injuries/VTE/aspiration to date; emotional support ongoing (reinforcing the expected recovery)

vitals.rr: 16/min, extubated, breathing comfortably with adequate FVC
vitals.temp: 36.8°C; extubated after sustained recovery of respiratory strength; regaining strength gradually (proximal → distal); transitioned to inpatient rehabilitation for continued PT/OT/speech; swallowing recovering (advancing diet with speech-therapy guidance); mood improving with the visible recovery + support; family educated on the gradual recovery course

labs.v: 42 y/o man R. Haddad presenting after ~5 days of progressive, SYMMETRIC, ASCENDING weakness: it began as tingling + weakness in both FEET and ascended to both legs (now unable to walk) and is now reaching his HANDS — ~2 WEEKS after a self-limited DIARRHEAL illness (presumed Campylobacter jejuni gastroenteritis, the classic GBS trigger). EXAM: symmetric FLACCID weakness (legs > arms, ascending), ABSENT deep tendon reflexes (AREFLEXIA — a hallmark), distal paresthesias, and—critically—FULLY INTACT alertness, cognition, and orientation (GBS does NOT affect consciousness or cognition). He is understandably anxious and frightened by how quickly he is losing function. PMH: previously healthy; the recent GI illness is the only notable antecedent. SOCIAL: married, works as an electrician, wife at bedside + frightened; no tobacco; values understanding what is happening to him. The CLINICAL PATTERN—acute, symmetric, ascending flaccid weakness with areflexia following an infection, in an alert patient—is classic for GUILLAIN-BARRÉ SYNDROME (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy). Because GBS can ASCEND to the respiratory muscles (causing neuromuscular respiratory failure—the leading cause of death) and can cause AUTONOMIC instability, he is admitted to a MONITORED setting for SERIAL respiratory mechanics, continuous cardiac + BP monitoring, diagnostic confirmation (LP, nerve conduction studies), disease-modifying therapy (IVIG or plasmapheresis), and comprehensive supportive care. DIFFERENTIATION: the symmetric ascending pattern + areflexia + post-infectious onset + preserved cognition distinguish GBS from stroke (focal, asymmetric, cognition/CN patterns), spinal cord compression (a sensory level + bowel/bladder + UMN signs), myasthenia gravis (fatigable weakness, ocular/bulbar onset, normal reflexes), and botulism/tick paralysis. The nurse's overriding priorities from the outset: anticipate RESPIRATORY failure via serial monitoring, watch for DYSAUTONOMIA, and begin the comprehensive supportive/comfort care an awake, progressively paralyzed patient needs.
labs.r: Acute, symmetric, ASCENDING flaccid weakness + AREFLEXIA following a (often diarrheal) infection, in a fully ALERT patient = Guillain-Barré syndrome. Key risks = ascending paralysis → neuromuscular RESPIRATORY FAILURE + AUTONOMIC instability. RN role: recognize the pattern, admit to monitoring, anticipate respiratory failure, and begin supportive care.

labs.n: Serial respiratory monitoring — anticipating neuromuscular respiratory failure
labs.v: The SINGLE most important nursing surveillance in GBS is for IMPENDING NEUROMUSCULAR RESPIRATORY FAILURE, because the ascending paralysis can reach the DIAPHRAGM and INTERCOSTAL muscles and is the leading cause of death—and because, unlike primary lung disease, the failure here is a PUMP (muscle) failure where OXYGENATION is preserved until LATE. MONITOR SERIAL BEDSIDE RESPIRATORY MECHANICS at scheduled intervals (e.g., q2-4h or more often if declining): FORCED VITAL CAPACITY (FVC) and NEGATIVE INSPIRATORY FORCE (NIF, a.k.a. maximal inspiratory pressure). WARNING THRESHOLDS/TRENDS indicating impending failure and the need to prepare for ELECTIVE intubation: a FALLING FVC—commonly cited as <15-20 mL/kg or an absolute <~1 L, or (the '20/30/40 rule') FVC <20 mL/kg, NIF weaker than -30 cm H2O, or maximal expiratory pressure <40—and especially a RAPID DOWNWARD TREND over hours. Additional bedside clues: rising RESPIRATORY RATE, shallow/rapid breathing, accessory-muscle use, inability to complete a full sentence or to count to a high number on a single breath (declining SINGLE-BREATH COUNT), a WEAK COUGH, difficulty handling SECRETIONS, restlessness/anxiety, and (LATE, ominous) paradoxical/abdominal breathing. CRITICAL TEACHING POINT: DO NOT wait for a falling SpO2 or a rising PaCO2 on ABG—these are LATE signs in neuromuscular failure; by the time oxygenation drops, the patient is in extremis. The correct approach is to intubate ELECTIVELY/SEMI-ELECTIVELY based on the declining mechanics and clinical trajectory, in a controlled manner, BEFORE a respiratory arrest. Keep airway/intubation equipment and a bag-valve-mask at the bedside, ensure ICU-level monitoring, assess SWALLOWING (bulbar weakness → aspiration risk; NPO + suction if unsafe), and escalate early to critical care/neurology. THIS PATIENT'S FVC/NIF trended down over hours with a weakening cough and secretion difficulty while his SpO2 was still 'fine' → he was transferred to the ICU and ELECTIVELY intubated before hypoxemia/arrest, exactly as intended.
labs.r: GBS's #1 killer is ascending paralysis causing NEUROMUSCULAR (pump) respiratory failure with oxygenation preserved until late. MONITOR serial FVC + NIF (+ single-breath count, cough, secretions, RR). Falling FVC (<15-20 mL/kg or <~1 L) / weak NIF / rapid downtrend → prepare for ELECTIVE intubation BEFORE hypoxemia or rising CO2 (LATE signs). Keep airway gear + BVM ready; escalate early. RN role: serial respiratory mechanics + anticipate/elective airway.

labs.v: AUTONOMIC DYSFUNCTION (DYSAUTONOMIA) is the SECOND major life-threat in GBS and requires continuous cardiac + BP monitoring: it causes LABILE BLOOD PRESSURE with wide swings (episodic HYPERTENSION and HYPOTENSION), CARDIAC ARRHYTHMIAS (sinus tachycardia is common; dangerous bradyarrhythmias—including profound bradycardia or asystole, sometimes provoked by tracheal SUCTIONING or vagal stimuli—can occur), as well as ILEUS, URINARY RETENTION, impaired sweating/temperature regulation, and SIADH. NURSING: continuous ECG + frequent BP; treat hemodynamic swings CAUTIOUSLY (these patients are sensitive—use short-acting agents and avoid overcorrection, since the next swing may be opposite); PRE-OXYGENATE and be cautious with suctioning (watch for bradycardia, have atropine available per protocol); monitor for ileus (bowel sounds, distension) and urinary retention (bladder scan/catheter); and monitor sodium (SIADH). DIAGNOSIS: GBS is primarily CLINICAL, supported by (1) LUMBAR PUNCTURE showing ALBUMINOCYTOLOGIC DISSOCIATION—an ELEVATED CSF PROTEIN with a NORMAL or near-normal white-cell COUNT (the protein may be normal in the first few days and rise over the first 1-2 weeks); and (2) NERVE CONDUCTION STUDIES/EMG showing demyelination (or axonal patterns in variants). DISEASE-MODIFYING TREATMENT (start EARLY in significantly affected/progressing patients): either IVIG (intravenous immunoglobulin, typically 2 g/kg over 5 days) OR PLASMAPHERESIS (plasma exchange)—the two are EQUALLY EFFECTIVE and are NOT used together (no added benefit); choice depends on availability, access, and patient factors (IVIG cautions: ensure adequate hydration, monitor renal function, infusion reactions, thrombosis, aseptic meningitis, and check IgA deficiency for anaphylaxis risk; plasmapheresis cautions: large-bore/central access, hypotension, citrate-induced hypocalcemia, coagulopathy, infection). CORTICOSTEROIDS are NOT effective in GBS (do not use them as monotherapy—an important and frequently tested distinction from other autoimmune/inflammatory conditions). Supportive care remains the backbone of management and the chief determinant of outcome. THIS PATIENT received IVIG and was monitored for dysautonomia (labile BP, suctioning-associated bradycardia).
labs.r: Dysautonomia = labile BP + arrhythmias (incl. suctioning-induced bradycardia/asystole) + ileus + urinary retention + SIADH → continuous cardiac/BP monitoring, cautious treatment of swings, careful suctioning (atropine available). Diagnosis: clinical + LP (albuminocytologic dissociation = high protein, normal cells) + NCS/EMG. Treat with IVIG OR plasmapheresis (equal, not combined); steroids NOT effective. RN role: monitor dysautonomia + deliver/monitor IVIG or plasma exchange.

labs.n: Comprehensive supportive care of an awake, paralyzed patient (Basic Care & Comfort)
labs.v: Because the GBS patient is typically FULLY AWAKE, ALERT, and able to FEEL (sensation + cognition intact) while progressively unable to MOVE or SPEAK, the bulk of nursing is intensive, comfort- and dignity-focused SUPPORTIVE CARE that also prevents the complications of immobility: (1) COMMUNICATION is paramount—establish a reliable system early (yes/no eye blinks, letter/picture boards, communication apps, mouthing) for a conscious patient who may be unable to talk (especially if ventilated); EXPLAIN every procedure and sensation, orient frequently, never discuss the patient as if absent, and ensure the call/alert system is within their control where possible; (2) PSYCHOLOGICAL/EMOTIONAL support—being awake and paralyzed (perhaps on a ventilator) is profoundly FRIGHTENING → provide frequent reassurance emphasizing that GBS is usually REVERSIBLE with expected recovery, reduce isolation, allow family presence, screen for and treat anxiety/depression, and involve psychology/chaplain; (3) RESPIRATORY/AIRWAY—serial FVC/NIF, suctioning with dysautonomia caution, aspiration precautions, ventilator + oral care if intubated; (4) IMMOBILITY complications—VTE/DVT PROPHYLAXIS (pharmacologic + sequential compression devices), PRESSURE-INJURY prevention (reposition q2h, pressure-redistributing surfaces, skin assessment, keep skin clean/dry), passive RANGE-OF-MOTION and proper POSITIONING/SPLINTS to prevent CONTRACTURES and FOOTDROP; (5) NUTRITION—assess SWALLOWING (bulbar weakness → high ASPIRATION risk → NPO + enteral feeding if unsafe), provide adequate calories/protein for healing, and manage constipation/ileus with a bowel regimen; (6) ELIMINATION—monitor for URINARY RETENTION (bladder scan, intermittent catheterization) and ileus; (7) PAIN—neuropathic pain and severe musculoskeletal/BACK pain are common and under-recognized → treat with gabapentin/pregabalin + analgesics + repositioning; (8) EYE CARE—facial/CN VII weakness causing incomplete eyelid closure → lubricating drops/ointment + protection to prevent corneal injury; (9) INFECTION prevention—ventilator-associated pneumonia, UTI, line and skin infections (bundles, hygiene); (10) MULTIDISCIPLINARY rehabilitation—PT/OT/SPEECH from early on, dietitian, social work, and a planned, often prolonged REHABILITATION course (recovery is gradual, proximal-to-distal). The nurse's comfort-and-communication-centered care, paired with vigilant prevention of immobility complications, is what carries an awake, paralyzed patient safely through the plateau to recovery.
labs.r: GBS patients are AWAKE + feeling while paralyzed → supportive care is central: COMMUNICATION system + explain everything + emotional reassurance (usually reversible); immobility prevention (VTE prophylaxis + SCDs, q2h repositioning/skin care, ROM + splints for contractures/footdrop); nutrition + swallow/aspiration assessment (enteral if unsafe); monitor urinary retention/ileus; treat neuropathic + back PAIN; eye care for facial weakness; infection prevention; PT/OT/speech rehab. RN role: comprehensive comfort + communication + immobility-complication prevention.

labs.v: PROGNOSIS: GBS is usually a MONOPHASIC illness—progression over up to ~4 weeks, a plateau, then GRADUAL recovery over weeks-to-months as myelin/nerves repair; recovery generally proceeds PROXIMAL-TO-DISTAL (roughly the reverse of the ascending onset). MOST patients recover substantially and many return to near-baseline, though some retain residual weakness, fatigue, or sensory symptoms; overall mortality is ~3-7%, driven by respiratory failure, dysautonomic arrhythmias, pulmonary embolism, sepsis/pneumonia, and other immobility complications—which is precisely why vigilant monitoring + supportive care matter so much. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct course + reassuring distinctions): COGNITION and CONSCIOUSNESS are INTACT (GBS does not affect the brain—so the awake patient must be communicated with and emotionally supported, never treated as unaware); the patient was NOT allowed to deteriorate to hypoxemia/arrest before securing the airway (elective intubation based on declining FVC/NIF—not waiting for a late SpO2 drop); CORTICOSTEROIDS were NOT used (ineffective in GBS); IVIG and plasmapheresis were NOT combined; NO pressure injury, VTE, aspiration pneumonia, or contractures developed (immobility complications prevented); dysautonomic swings were managed cautiously (no overcorrection) and suctioning-induced bradycardia was anticipated; this is NOT a stroke/cord compression/myasthenia (symmetric ascending areflexic post-infectious pattern with intact cognition). PROTECTIVE FACTORS: early recognition of the GBS pattern + admission to MONITORING with serial respiratory mechanics; ELECTIVE, controlled intubation before a crisis; early IVIG; meticulous supportive/immobility care preventing complications; continuous dysautonomia monitoring; a strong COMMUNICATION + emotional-support plan for an awake patient; engaged family; and early multidisciplinary REHABILITATION. EXPECTED trajectory (as here): controlled support through the paralytic plateau, then steady recovery of respiratory strength and motor function (proximal-to-distal), ventilator weaning + extubation, and rehabilitation toward functional recovery. The case underscores that GBS outcomes hinge on ANTICIPATING respiratory failure (serial FVC/NIF, intubate before hypoxemia), monitoring dysautonomia, and delivering comprehensive, comfort- and communication-centered supportive care to a fully conscious, paralyzed patient.
labs.r: GBS is usually monophasic with gradual proximal-to-distal recovery; most recover substantially (mortality ~3-7% from respiratory/autonomic/immobility complications). Negatives confirm correct care: intact cognition (communicate!), airway secured electively before hypoxemia, no steroids, IVIG/plasmapheresis not combined, immobility complications prevented, dysautonomia managed cautiously. Protective: early recognition + monitoring + elective intubation + IVIG + supportive care + rehab. RN role: anticipate respiratory failure + monitor dysautonomia + comprehensive supportive care.

nursesNote: Admission — 42 y/o man R. Haddad with ~5 days of progressive SYMMETRIC ASCENDING weakness: began as tingling + weakness in both FEET → both legs (cannot walk) → now reaching both HANDS, ~2 WEEKS after a self-limited DIARRHEAL illness (presumed Campylobacter). EXAM: symmetric flaccid weakness (legs>arms, ascending), AREFLEXIA (absent DTRs), distal paresthesias, FULLY ALERT + intact cognition (GBS spares the brain); anxious/frightened by rapid progression. PMH: healthy. SOCIAL: married, electrician, wife at bedside. Pattern = GUILLAIN-BARRÉ SYNDROME (AIDP). Admitted to MONITORED bed (continuous cardiac + SpO2; airway/intubation gear + BVM at bedside) for serial respiratory mechanics, dysautonomia monitoring, diagnosis (LP, NCS/EMG), IVIG, and comprehensive supportive care. ADMISSION: alert, anxious, leg + early hand weakness, areflexia; baseline FORCED VITAL CAPACITY (FVC) + NEGATIVE INSPIRATORY FORCE (NIF) + single-breath count documented as the TREND baseline; swallow screened; VS HR 92, BP 138/84, RR 20, SpO2 98% RA. HOUR 8: weakness ascended (mid-thigh + both hands); SERIAL FVC/NIF TRENDING DOWN + single-breath count decreasing + WEAKER COUGH + secretion-clearance difficulty, while SpO2 still 96% (KEY: falling mechanics precede hypoxemia — do NOT wait for SpO2 drop or rising CO2, which are LATE) → neurology + critical care notified, airway readiness confirmed, ICU transfer for likely ELECTIVE intubation; IVIG initiated; cardiac monitoring for dysautonomia (BP labile 150/90 ↔ 110/64). ICU: ELECTIVELY intubated/ventilated for neuromuscular (pump) respiratory failure anticipated from the declining FVC/NIF + weak cough — BEFORE hypoxemia/arrest; patient fully AWAKE + able to feel → COMMUNICATION method established (eye blinks/board) + everything explained + never spoken about as if absent; continuous cardiac + BP monitoring; CAUTION with suctioning (transient bradycardia noted — autonomic; atropine available; preoxygenate); IVIG continuing. Diagnosis supported by LP ALBUMINOCYTOLOGIC DISSOCIATION (high CSF protein, normal cells) + NCS. NO corticosteroids (ineffective in GBS); IVIG + plasmapheresis NOT combined. ICU DAY 3 (plateau, near-complete tetraparesis but fully conscious): comprehensive Basic-Care-&-Comfort in full effect — VTE prophylaxis + SCDs; q2h repositioning + skin/pressure-injury surveillance; passive ROM + splints (prevent contractures/footdrop); enteral nutrition (unsafe swallow — aspiration precautions); bowel/bladder management (monitor ileus + urinary retention); neuropathic + BACK PAIN controlled (gabapentin + analgesia + repositioning); eye lubrication for facial weakness; consistent COMMUNICATION + frequent reassurance (GBS usually REVERSIBLE) + family presence; infection prevention (VAP/UTI bundles). ICU DAY 10 (early recovery): FVC/NIF trending UP + return of proximal movement (recovery proximal→distal, reverse of onset); progressing ventilator weaning trials; NO pressure injury/VTE/aspiration; emotional support ongoing. WEEK 4: extubated after sustained respiratory-strength recovery; regaining strength proximal→distal; transitioned to inpatient REHAB (PT/OT/speech); swallowing recovering (diet advanced per speech therapy); mood improving with visible recovery; family educated on the gradual recovery course. Throughout: anticipate respiratory failure (serial FVC/NIF → elective airway), monitor dysautonomia, and deliver comfort- and communication-centered care to an awake, paralyzed patient.
providerOrders: ADMIT to a monitored (step-down/ICU-capable) bed with continuous cardiac + SpO2 monitoring; Guillain-Barré syndrome
DIAGNOSIS: Guillain-Barré syndrome (AIDP) — ascending flaccid weakness + areflexia post-diarrheal illness; monitor for respiratory failure + dysautonomia
CODE STATUS: full code; alert with intact capacity (GBS spares cognition); wife involved
RESPIRATORY SURVEILLANCE (highest priority):

Serial bedside FORCED VITAL CAPACITY (FVC) and NEGATIVE INSPIRATORY FORCE (NIF) q2-4h (more often if declining); document single-breath count, cough strength, RR, accessory-muscle use, secretion handling
Prepare for ELECTIVE intubation for falling FVC (<15-20 mL/kg or <~1 L), weakening NIF (worse than -30 cm H2O), or rapid downtrend — do NOT wait for SpO2 drop or rising PaCO2 (late signs)
Keep intubation equipment + bag-valve-mask at bedside; ICU transfer threshold defined; escalate early to neurology/critical care
Aspiration precautions; assess swallowing (bulbar weakness) — NPO + suction setup if unsafe; HOB elevated
AUTONOMIC (DYSAUTONOMIA) MONITORING:
Continuous ECG + frequent BP; anticipate labile BP (hyper/hypotension) + arrhythmias
Treat hemodynamic swings cautiously with short-acting agents (avoid overcorrection); pre-oxygenate + caution with suctioning (bradycardia/asystole risk) — atropine available per protocol
Monitor for ileus (bowel sounds/distension), urinary retention (bladder scan/catheterize), and SIADH (serum sodium)
DIAGNOSTICS:
Lumbar puncture (albuminocytologic dissociation — elevated CSF protein, normal cell count); nerve conduction studies/EMG
Baseline labs; for IVIG: renal function + IgA level (deficiency → anaphylaxis risk) + hydration
DISEASE-MODIFYING TREATMENT (start early):
IVIG (e.g., 2 g/kg over 5 days) OR plasmapheresis — equally effective; do NOT combine; choose per access/availability/patient factors

IVIG monitoring: infusion reactions, ensure hydration, renal function, thrombosis, aseptic meningitis; Plasmapheresis monitoring: central access, hypotension, hypocalcemia (citrate), coagulopathy, infection
Do NOT give corticosteroids (ineffective in GBS)
SUPPORTIVE / BASIC CARE & COMFORT (mainstay):
COMMUNICATION: establish a reliable method (eye blinks/letter-picture board/device) — patient is awake; explain all care; never speak as if absent; orient frequently
PSYCHOLOGICAL support: frequent reassurance (GBS usually reversible/recovers), reduce isolation, family presence, screen/treat anxiety/depression, psychology/chaplain

Immobility prevention: VTE prophylaxis (pharmacologic + sequential compression devices); reposition q2h + pressure-injury surveillance + support surfaces; passive ROM + splints (prevent contractures/footdrop)
Nutrition: dietitian; enteral feeding if swallow unsafe; adequate calories/protein; bowel regimen for constipation/ileus
Pain: treat neuropathic + musculoskeletal/back pain (gabapentin/pregabalin + analgesics + repositioning)
Eye care: lubrication/protection if facial weakness causes incomplete lid closure
Infection prevention: VAP bundle if ventilated, UTI/line/skin care
MULTIDISCIPLINARY + REHAB:
Neurology + critical care + respiratory therapy + PT/OT/speech therapy + dietitian + social work; early rehabilitation; plan for prolonged, gradual recovery
FAMILY + EDUCATION:

Educate patient + family on the expected monophasic course (progression → plateau → gradual proximal-to-distal recovery) and the rationale for monitoring/elective ventilation; involve family in communication + support
DISCHARGE/TRANSITION:
Transition to inpatient rehabilitation when respiratory status stable + extubated; ongoing PT/OT/speech; outpatient neurology follow-up; education on recovery + residual-symptom management

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters in this ascending-weakness presentation?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: Acute, symmetric, ASCENDING flaccid weakness with AREFLEXIA following a recent diarrheal illness, in a fully alert patient — the classic pattern of Guillain-Barré syndrome
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognizing the GBS pattern drives monitoring + treatment. RN role: pattern recognition + admission to monitoring.

opts.text: Risk of ascending paralysis reaching the respiratory muscles — serial FVC and NIF must be monitored to anticipate neuromuscular respiratory failure (the leading cause of death)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — respiratory failure is the #1 killer; serial mechanics anticipate it. RN role: serial FVC/NIF + airway readiness.

opts.text: A falling FVC/NIF, weakening cough, difficulty clearing secretions, and a declining single-breath count signal impending respiratory failure even when SpO2 is still normal
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — neuromuscular failure preserves oxygenation until late; trends matter. RN role: act on declining mechanics, not late hypoxemia.

opts.text: Autonomic dysfunction (labile BP, arrhythmias including suctioning-induced bradycardia, ileus, urinary retention) — requires continuous cardiac + BP monitoring
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — dysautonomia is the second major life-threat. RN role: continuous cardiac/BP monitoring + cautious management.

opts.text: The patient is fully awake, alert, and able to feel despite the paralysis — communication, explanation, and emotional support are essential
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — GBS spares cognition; the awake, paralyzed patient must be communicated with + supported. RN role: communication + psychological support.

opts.text: Immobility complications (VTE, pressure injury, contractures/footdrop, aspiration) must be prevented from the outset
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — prolonged paralysis risks immobility complications. RN role: VTE prophylaxis + skin care + ROM/splints + aspiration precautions.

opts.text: Bulbar involvement (dysphagia, weak cough, facial weakness) raises aspiration risk and the need to assess swallowing and protect the eyes
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — bulbar weakness → aspiration + corneal-exposure risk. RN role: swallow assessment + aspiration precautions + eye care.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed (relevant for IVIG; also check IgA level)
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; IgA deficiency raises IVIG anaphylaxis risk. Relevant but not the central cue.

opts.text: Because his SpO2 is 96-98% on room air, his respiratory status is reassuring and respiratory monitoring can be spaced out
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. In neuromuscular (pump) failure, OXYGENATION is preserved until LATE — a normal SpO2 is falsely reassuring. Monitor serial FVC/NIF closely; a normal SpO2 does not rule out impending respiratory failure.

opts.text: His altered ability to move means his cognition is impaired, so detailed explanations are unnecessary
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. GBS does NOT affect cognition or consciousness — the patient is fully aware and frightened. He needs clear communication, explanation of all care, and emotional support; never treat him as unaware.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: GBS is an immune-mediated polyradiculoneuropathy, often post-infectious (e.g., Campylobacter), causing ascending symmetric flaccid weakness + areflexia with intact cognition
opts.rat: CORRECT — pathophysiology + presentation. Foundation for recognition.

opts.text: Ascending paralysis can cause neuromuscular respiratory (pump) failure with oxygenation preserved until late → monitor serial FVC/NIF and intubate electively on declining mechanics, not on a late SpO2/CO2 change
opts.rat: CORRECT — respiratory-failure monitoring principle. Foundation for the airway plan.

opts.text: Dysautonomia causes labile BP and arrhythmias (including suctioning-induced bradycardia) → continuous monitoring, cautious treatment of swings, and careful suctioning
opts.rat: CORRECT — dysautonomia principle. Foundation for cardiac/BP monitoring.

opts.text: Diagnosis is clinical + LP showing albuminocytologic dissociation (high protein, normal cells) + nerve conduction studies
opts.rat: CORRECT — diagnostic framework. Foundation for confirmation.

opts.text: Treatment is IVIG OR plasmapheresis (equally effective, not combined); corticosteroids are NOT effective in GBS; supportive care is the mainstay
opts.rat: CORRECT — treatment principle. Foundation for therapy decisions.

opts.text: Because the patient is awake and feeling, supportive care centers on communication, emotional support, pain control, and prevention of immobility complications
opts.rat: CORRECT — supportive-care principle. Foundation for comprehensive nursing care.

opts.text: GBS typically affects cognition and consciousness, so a declining level of consciousness is an expected part of the disease
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. GBS is a peripheral nerve disorder that does NOT affect the brain — cognition and consciousness are intact. A declining LOC is NOT from GBS itself and would prompt evaluation for another cause (e.g., hypercapnia, another process).

opts.text: High-dose corticosteroids are the first-line treatment for GBS, like for most autoimmune conditions
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Corticosteroids are NOT effective in GBS (a frequently tested distinction). The disease-modifying treatments are IVIG or plasmapheresis.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Respiratory status r/t ascending paralysis — serial FVC/NIF, monitor cough/secretions/single-breath count, keep airway gear ready, and prepare for elective intubation before hypoxemia
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Neuromuscular respiratory failure is the leading cause of death; anticipating it is paramount. RN role: respiratory surveillance + airway readiness.

opts.text: Autonomic instability r/t dysautonomia — continuous cardiac + BP monitoring, cautious management of swings, careful suctioning (bradycardia risk)
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Dysautonomic arrhythmias/BP swings can be life-threatening. RN role: cardiac/BP monitoring + cautious intervention.

opts.text: Aspiration + nutrition r/t bulbar weakness — assess swallowing, aspiration precautions, NPO + enteral feeding if unsafe
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Aspiration is a real risk with bulbar involvement and worsens respiratory status. RN role: swallow assessment + aspiration precautions + nutrition.

opts.text: Immobility-complication prevention r/t paralysis — VTE prophylaxis, pressure-injury prevention (q2h repositioning/skin care), ROM + splints for contractures/footdrop
opts.rat: #4 — Significant over the prolonged course. RN role: prevent VTE/pressure injury/contractures.

opts.text: Communication + psychological support r/t an awake, paralyzed, frightened patient — establish a communication method, explain all care, reassure (usually reversible), involve family
opts.rat: #5 — Essential throughout. The awake patient's comfort/dignity/coping depend on it. RN role: communication + emotional support.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this patient with GBS.

opts.text: Monitor serial FVC and NIF, keep intubation equipment + a bag-valve-mask at the bedside, and prepare for elective intubation if mechanics decline
opts.rat: Core GBS respiratory surveillance + airway readiness. RN role: serial mechanics + elective-airway preparation.

opts.text: Continuously monitor ECG and BP, treat hemodynamic swings cautiously, and pre-oxygenate + use caution with suctioning (have atropine available)
opts.rat: Appropriate dysautonomia monitoring/management. RN role: cardiac/BP monitoring + careful suctioning.

opts.text: Assess swallowing, institute aspiration precautions, and provide enteral nutrition if the swallow is unsafe
opts.rat: Protects against aspiration + maintains nutrition with bulbar weakness. RN role: swallow assessment + nutrition.

opts.text: Provide VTE prophylaxis + sequential compression devices, reposition q2h with skin surveillance, and perform passive ROM with splints to prevent contractures/footdrop
opts.rat: Prevents immobility complications. RN role: VTE/skin/contracture prevention.

opts.text: Establish a reliable communication method, explain all care, and provide frequent reassurance that GBS is usually reversible; involve family
opts.rat: Essential support for an awake, paralyzed patient. RN role: communication + emotional support.

opts.text: Administer IVIG (or arrange plasmapheresis) with appropriate monitoring; treat neuropathic/back pain; provide eye care for facial weakness
opts.rat: Disease-modifying therapy + symptom/comfort care. RN role: IVIG/plasmapheresis + pain + eye care.

opts.text: Rely on continuous pulse oximetry alone to detect respiratory failure and space out FVC/NIF checks while SpO2 is normal
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — in neuromuscular failure SpO2 stays normal until late; serial FVC/NIF must continue closely. SpO2 alone misses impending failure.

opts.text: Administer high-dose corticosteroids as the primary treatment for GBS
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — corticosteroids are ineffective in GBS. Use IVIG or plasmapheresis.

opts.text: Give both IVIG and plasmapheresis together to maximize the treatment effect
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — combining them provides no added benefit; choose one (they are equally effective).

opts.text: Since the patient cannot move or speak, limit explanations and proceed with care quickly without engaging him
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — he is fully awake and aware; failing to communicate is distressing and a breach of dignity. Establish communication + explain all care.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should act. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Monitor serial FVC/NIF + respiratory status, keep airway equipment ready, and escalate/prepare for elective intubation as mechanics decline
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Anticipating/securing the airway prevents the leading cause of death. RN role: respiratory surveillance + airway.

opts.text: Establish continuous cardiac + BP monitoring for dysautonomia and use caution with suctioning
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Dysautonomia is the next life-threat. RN role: cardiac/BP monitoring + careful suctioning.

opts.text: Assess swallowing + institute aspiration precautions; support diagnosis (LP/NCS) and initiate IVIG (or plasmapheresis)
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Protect the airway from aspiration + start disease-modifying therapy. RN role: swallow/aspiration + IVIG/plasmapheresis.

opts.text: Implement immobility-complication prevention (VTE prophylaxis, q2h repositioning/skin care, ROM/splints) and pain/eye care
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Prevent the complications of prolonged paralysis. RN role: immobility prevention + comfort.

opts.text: Establish a communication method, explain all care, provide reassurance + family support, and engage rehabilitation early
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing/foundational. Supports the awake patient + recovery. RN role: communication + emotional support + rehab.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Hour 8: serial FVC/NIF trending down with a weakening cough and secretion difficulty (SpO2 still 96%) → recognized as impending respiratory failure, ICU transfer + elective intubation arranged before hypoxemia
opts.rat: Correct anticipation of neuromuscular respiratory failure from declining mechanics (not waiting for late hypoxemia) — model handling that prevents a crisis.

opts.text: ICU: electively intubated in a controlled manner before arrest; awake patient given a communication method with all care explained; IVIG underway; dysautonomia monitored
opts.rat: Controlled airway + communication + disease-modifying therapy + monitoring = correct comprehensive management. Foundation for safe passage through the plateau.

opts.text: ICU day 3 (plateau): comprehensive supportive care delivered — VTE prophylaxis, q2h repositioning, ROM/splints, enteral nutrition, pain control, eye care, reassurance — with no pressure injury, VTE, or aspiration
opts.rat: Immobility complications prevented + comfort maintained through the paralytic plateau. Correct supportive care.

opts.text: ICU day 10: FVC/NIF trending up with return of proximal movement (proximal-to-distal recovery), progressing through ventilator weaning
opts.rat: Expected recovery pattern (proximal-to-distal, reverse of onset) with improving respiratory strength. Recovery underway.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — the team monitored only SpO2 (which stayed normal) and spaced out FVC/NIF checks; the patient suddenly became unable to breathe and arrested before the airway was secured
opts.rat: ⚠️ Relying on SpO2 missed neuromuscular failure (oxygenation preserved until late) → respiratory arrest. Demonstrates why serial FVC/NIF + elective intubation matter.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — high-dose corticosteroids were given as the primary treatment and IVIG was delayed; no benefit was seen and the course was prolonged
opts.rat: ⚠️ Corticosteroids are ineffective in GBS; using them in place of IVIG/plasmapheresis delays effective treatment. Demonstrates the steroid distinction.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — vigorous tracheal suctioning without precautions triggered profound bradycardia/asystole due to unrecognized dysautonomia
opts.rat: ⚠️ Dysautonomia + vagal stimulation from suctioning caused a dangerous bradyarrhythmia. Demonstrates the need for cautious suctioning + cardiac monitoring + available atropine.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — staff treated the immobile patient as if unaware, performing care silently without communication; he became severely anxious and felt trapped and dehumanized
opts.rat: ⚠️ GBS spares cognition — failing to communicate with an awake, paralyzed patient causes profound distress. Demonstrates the necessity of a communication method + explanation + support.

opts.text: During the plateau phase, the degree of weakness is essentially stable day-to-day (neither ascending further nor yet recovering) while supportive care continues
opts.rat: The plateau phase between progression and recovery shows stable deficits — expected; continue supportive care and monitor for the transition to recovery.

opts.text: Week 4: extubated after sustained respiratory recovery, regaining strength proximal-to-distal, transitioned to inpatient rehabilitation with improving mood
opts.rat: Sustained recovery + rehabilitation transition. Standard expected trajectory for monophasic GBS.
